text: The surgeon uses an optical trocar to safely enter the abdominal cavity, cleans the scope, and places additional trocars. The DaVinci robotic system is then brought in for docking.

Alright, I'm sitting at the console so we can start our sleeve gastrectomy. And currently our pressure setting is at fifteen. I want you to go ahead and drop the pressure setting down to ten since we have the new air seal caps.
The surgeon begins the robotic sleeve gastrectomy with a 36 French bougie in place, using a bipolar vessel sealer to access the lesser sac.

The surgeon begins the dissection at the inferior portion of the stomach, creating a window and working distally, aiming to carry the dissection to a point between four and six centimeters from the pylorus.

The surgeon identifies the pylorus and measures approximately six centimeters from it, using the insessura as a main landmark. The stomach is observed to take a sharp turn, prompting careful consideration to avoid distal narrowing.
The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer to dissect the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, mobilizing this section in preparation for creating the sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon approaches the top part of the stomach, noting the presence of a hiatal hernia. The stomach is being fully mobilized to facilitate the creation of a better sleeve gastrectomy.
The surgeon exposes the left diaphragm, ensuring complete visualization beyond the short gastric vessels. The posterior attachments are released, and the stomach appears well-mobilized.

The surgeon positions the bougie along the stomach to guide the creation of the sleeve gastrectomy. A blue staple load is inserted into the stapler, clamped onto the tissue, allowing edema to reduce before engaging the stapler to begin stapling from the chosen starting point upwards.

The surgeon applies compression to the stomach and fires the stapler, using a combination of staple loads to complete the sleeve gastrectomy. The stapling progresses smoothly without leaving excess pouching, and the sleeve is formed close to the diaphragm.

The surgeon achieves perfect visualization with even gas pressure, revealing the hiatus. The sleeve gastrectomy appears well-formed, with minimal excess tissue or 'dog ear' left at the top of the stomach.

The surgeon maintains visualization while removing the trocar to extract the stomach specimen. The operating table is adjusted to fifteen degrees for a leak test. The bougie is removed, revealing a uniform sleeve gastrectomy. Minor hemostasis is applied at the top of the sleeve.
The surgeon applies fibrin sealant to the completed 36 French sleeve gastrectomy, which was performed without an assist port at a pressure of ten. The sleeve appears uniform and well-formed.
The surgeon removes the trocar and uses a Kelly clamp to dilate the hole, maintaining visualization with the aid of an air seal despite the normal pneumoperitoneum.

That's the robotic sleeve gastrectomy using the new air seal system, which integrate with the da Vinci trocars. We did this at a lower pressure than we normally use and with no assist port. So I thought it was a great procedure.
